metadata.title: UPSC Mains GS Paper 2 - Governance & Social Justice Comprehensive Syllabus
metadata.purpose: Complete syllabus for MentorsDaily UPSC portal

second_arc_reports.report_1: Good Governance and Probity in Public Life
second_arc_reports.report_2: Refurbishing of Personnel Administration - A Philosophy of Governance

second_arc_reports.report_8: Combating Terrorism and Use of Force
second_arc_reports.report_10: Financing of Political Parties and Election Commission

topics.primary_source: 2nd ARC Report 1: Good Governance and Probity in Public Life - Recommendations on participatory, transparent, accountable governance structures
key_concepts: Participation - all stakeholders in decision-making processes
Rule of law - impartial legal framework and enforcement
Transparency - open information access and decision clarity
Responsiveness - timely service delivery addressing citizen needs
Consensus orientation - mediation of diverse interests
Equity and inclusiveness - all groups represented especially marginalized
Effectiveness and efficiency - achieving objectives with optimal resource use
Accountability - decision-makers answerable to public and institutions
Distinction from governance - governance is broader institutional framework
Local governance application - PESA 1996, scheduled areas democratic governance
Devolution principles - 73rd/74th Amendment implementation
Inter-relationship between characteristics - synergistic effect needed
India's governance gaps - bureaucratic discretion, corruption, capacity constraints
Reform measures - RTI, CAG oversight, parliamentary committees
Citizen engagement - increasing participation through e-platforms

scheme_1.name: National e-Governance Plan (NeGP)
scheme_1.objective: Leverage ICT for governance transformation across center, states, districts
scheme_1.status: Ongoing - Mission Mode Projects shifted to Digital India

scheme_2.name: Digital India Programme
scheme_2.objective: 9 pillars - broadband connectivity, capacity building, e-governance
scheme_2.status: Active - integrated with government service delivery

issues_challenges: Persistence of discretionary power in bureaucracy
Corruption and rent-seeking behavior despite reforms
Low awareness among citizens about participation mechanisms
Accountability gaps when multiple agencies involved
Resource constraints in implementing governance reforms
reforms_needed: Simplify procedures for citizen grievance redressal
Capacity building of public officials on governance principles
Decentralization of authority with accountability mechanisms
Integration of e-governance with ground-level service delivery
mains_answer_angles: 8 pillars of good governance and India's implementation status
Good governance as prerequisite for development and citizen welfare
Role of transparency and accountability in fighting corruption
e-Governance as enabler of good governance principles
Challenges in establishing participatory governance in federal structure

topics.topic_name: New Public Management vs New Public Service Paradigms
topics.primary_source: 2nd ARC Report 2: Refurbishing Personnel Administration - Philosophy of Governance - Contrast between service-delivery and citizen-centric approaches
key_concepts: New Public Management - market mechanisms, efficiency, customer service orientation
NPM critique - reduced equity focus, privatization pressures, citizen as customer
New Public Service - restoration of democratic values and citizenship
NPS principles - public interest, democratic governance, social equity
Public service motivation - intrinsic drivers beyond financial incentives
Separation of politics and administration - limitations and interdependence
Hollowing out thesis - erosion of public sector capability through privatization
Citizen participation vs consumer choice - fundamental difference in approach
Implementation in Indian context - hybrid model necessity
Performance management - output vs outcome measures tension
Accountability mechanisms - different under NPM vs NPS
Public administration reform - learning from global experiences
Civil service values - neutrality, impartiality, public interest
Service delivery quality - standardization vs customization

scheme_1.name: Sevottam (Citizen Service Engagement) Model
scheme_1.objective: Implement citizen charter and service standards across government
scheme_1.status: Active - adopted by multiple ministries and states

scheme_2.name: Mission Karmayogi
scheme_2.objective: Competency-based human resource capacity building for civil servants
scheme_2.status: Ongoing - integrated learning platform iGOT Karmayogi launched

issues_challenges: Tension between efficiency and equity objectives
Market-based approaches reducing social protection focus
Decline in permanent bureaucracy morale and retention
Blurred accountability in outsourced service delivery
Resistance to organizational culture change in public agencies
reforms_needed: Integrate NPS principles with performance metrics
Strengthen democratic control over market mechanisms
Enhance public service motivation through values-based training
Balance efficiency with equity and social welfare objectives
mains_answer_angles: Strengths and weaknesses of NPM approach in Indian governance
Relevance of NPS principles for democratic India
Hybrid models combining best of both approaches
Impact of paradigm shifts on civil service culture
Citizen participation vs market efficiency tensions

topics.topic_name: Regulatory Governance - TRAI, CERC, IRDAI, SEBI
topics.primary_source: 2nd ARC Report 1: Good Governance - Institutional framework for independent regulation protecting stakeholder interests
key_concepts: Regulatory agencies - need for independence from government and regulated entities
TRAI (Telecom Regulatory Authority of India) - 1997 - spectrum allocation, tariff regulation
CERC (Central Electricity Regulatory Commission) - 1998 - tariff, licensing, dispute resolution
IRDAI (Insurance Regulatory Development Authority) - 1999 - market conduct, consumer protection
SEBI (Securities Exchange Board of India) - 1988 - investor protection, market integrity
Board composition - independence criteria, conflict of interest provisions
Accountability mechanisms - parliamentary oversight, judicial review, transparency
Regulatory capture risk - regulated entities influencing regulators
Multi-stakeholder approach - balancing industry, consumer, government interests
Dispute resolution powers - quasi-judicial authority of regulators
Tariff-setting principles - cost-plus methodology vs competitive pricing
Consumer grievance redressal - robust mechanisms and OMBUDSMAN offices
Sectoral variations - telecom open competition vs electricity regulated tariff
International coordination - convergence with global regulatory practices

scheme_1.name: TRAI Regulatory Framework
scheme_1.objective: Autonomous regulation of telecom sector, spectrum management, consumer protection
scheme_1.status: Active - recent focus on 5G rollout, spectrum auctions, net neutrality

scheme_2.name: CERC Regulatory Framework
scheme_2.objective: Federal regulation of interstate electricity transactions and tariffs
scheme_2.status: Active - renewables integration, smart grid development

scheme_3.name: IRDAI Market Conduct Framework
scheme_3.objective: Protection of policyholders, grievance redressal, regulatory innovation
scheme_3.status: Recent reforms - health insurance regulations, Bima Sugam platform

issues_challenges: Regulatory capture by powerful industry players
Conflict between pro-consumer and pro-industry objectives
Limited appeal mechanisms against regulatory decisions
Political pressure on independent regulatory decisions
reforms_needed: Strengthen independence guarantees through statutory provisions
Improve technical expertise of regulatory officials
Establish clear appellate processes with reasonable timelines
mains_answer_angles: Role of independent regulatory agencies in market governance
Balancing independence with accountability and transparency
Sectoral regulatory frameworks - comparison across telecom, power, insurance, securities
Consumer protection mechanisms in regulated sectors
Challenges of regulatory capture and mitigation strategies

topics.primary_source: 2nd ARC Report 1: Good Governance and Probity - Identifies systemic gaps in transparency, accountability, service delivery
key_concepts: Definition - gap between governance principles and ground-level implementation
Institutional weaknesses - overlapping jurisdictions, accountability gaps
Discretionary power abuse - inadequate checks on bureaucratic discretion
Corruption manifestations - petty to systemic, endemic to sectors
Service delivery failures - delays, quality issues, exclusion of marginalized
Transparency deficit - information withholding, opaque decision processes
Participation deficit - limited citizen engagement in governance
Accountability gap - weak mechanisms for punishing officials
Resource constraints - insufficient capacity for reform implementation
Coordination failure - fragmented approach across government levels
Capacity deficit - skill gaps in public administration
Trust deficit - erosion of citizen confidence in institutions
Electoral malpractices - money and muscle power in elections
Organizational dysfunction - red tape, bureaucratic inertia

scheme_1.name: Right to Information Act 2005
scheme_1.objective: Enhance transparency and accountability through information access
scheme_1.status: Active - over 50 lakh RTI applications filed annually

scheme_2.name: e-Governance initiatives
scheme_2.objective: Use technology to reduce discretion, improve efficiency, transparency
scheme_2.status: Ongoing - rapid expansion through Digital India Programme

issues_challenges: Systemic nature - deficits rooted in institutional design
Multiple causes - capacity, incentives, accountability structures
Resistance to change - vested interests in existing systems
Resource constraints - limited funding for reforms
reforms_needed: Enhance transparency through e-governance and RTI
Develop robust accountability mechanisms - Lokpal, CAG
Improve service delivery design - citizen-centric approach
Invest in capacity building - training, technology, infrastructure
mains_answer_angles: Nature and extent of governance deficit in Indian context
Root causes - institutional, behavioral, structural factors
Manifestations across sectors - health, education, infrastructure
Impact on development outcomes and social welfare
Strategies for addressing governance deficit - comprehensive approach needed

topics.topic_name: National e-Governance Plan - Components and Implementation
topics.primary_source: 2nd ARC Report 15: e-Governance for Improved Service Delivery - Framework for integrating ICT across governance
key_concepts: NeGP pillars - mission mode projects, service delivery, infrastructure
Mission Mode Projects - core e-governance implementations across sectors
Service delivery portals - single-window systems, integrated databases
Infrastructure layer - broadband connectivity, data centers, security
Capacity building - digital literacy, skill development programs
Legal framework - Digital Signature Act, IT Act 2000, data protection
G2C services - citizen access to government services online
G2B services - business registration, licensing, compliance
G2E services - employee management, pension systems
G2G services - inter-government communication and coordination
Open Government Data - public access to government datasets
Standards and interoperability - ensuring system connectivity
Security and cybersecurity - protection of government and citizen data
Change management - transition from paper to digital processes

scheme_1.name: National e-Governance Plan
scheme_1.objective: Transform governance through ICT - reduce corruption, improve efficiency, enhance service delivery

scheme_2.name: Digital India - 9 Pillars
scheme_2.objective: Broadband, digital literacy, e-governance, e-services, public database, cybersecurity

scheme_3.name: Government e-Marketplace (GeM)
scheme_3.objective: Online procurement platform eliminating middlemen, ensuring transparency
scheme_3.status: Active - over 50 lakh vendors, transactions exceeding 5 lakh crores

issues_challenges: Digital divide - rural areas lacking connectivity infrastructure
Adoption barriers - resistance from officials, low digital literacy
Legacy systems incompatibility - difficulty in data migration
Cybersecurity vulnerabilities - data breaches and unauthorized access
Last-mile delivery gaps - technology not reaching poorest beneficiaries
reforms_needed: Expand broadband connectivity to underserved areas
Mandatory digital literacy programs for officials and citizens
Strengthen cybersecurity frameworks and regular audits
Integrate offline service delivery with online systems
mains_answer_angles: e-Governance as tool for reducing corruption and improving transparency
Role of technology in enhancing service delivery efficiency
Digital India integration with e-governance objectives
Challenges of implementing technology-driven governance in India
Citizens' rights and data protection in digital governance systems

topics.topic_name: IAS, IPS, IFS - Recruitment, Training, Career Structure
topics.primary_source: 2nd ARC Report 2: Refurbishing Personnel Administration - Cadre Management recommendations for civil service excellence
key_concepts: UPSC recruitment - eligibility, CSE examination pattern, selection process
IAS cadre - generalist civil service, all-India deployment, hierarchy
IPS cadre - law enforcement, paramilitary command, specialized units
IFS cadre - diplomatic service, international negotiations, cultural representation
Training institutions - LBSNAA, NIMA, FIA for foundational courses
In-service training - mid-career courses, specialized programs, foreign deputation
Seniority systems - promotion based on seniority with merit assessment
Career progression - from Group A to senior positions, lateral moves possible
Deputation - temporary transfer to other organizations for experience
Superannuation rules - retirement at 60 years, extension provisions
Performance appraisal - APAR system, confidential reports
Separation from service - grounds, procedures, appeals mechanism
Cadre management - state cadre, inter-cadre movements
Emoluments and benefits - pay scales, allowances, pension

scheme_1.name: Civil Services Examination
scheme_1.objective: Competitive selection of meritorious candidates for civil services
scheme_1.status: Annual - over 10 lakh aspirants, multiple attempts allowed

scheme_2.name: Lal Bahadur Shastri National Academy of Administration
scheme_2.objective: Foundational training for IAS officers, inculcate public service values
scheme_2.status: Ongoing - curriculum reformed regularly, international exposure

issues_challenges: Generalist-specialist debate - whether generalists adequate for complex governance
Seniority vs merit tension - inflexible promotion systems
Representation gaps - dominated by certain social groups
Motivation decline - poor morale, brain drain to private sector
Accountability gaps - difficult to hold senior officials accountable
reforms_needed: Introduce functional specialization in civil service careers
Strengthen merit-based performance appraisal and promotion
Increase diversity through targeted recruitment and training
mains_answer_angles: Civil service as backbone of good governance
Recruitment, training, career management - current system and reforms
Challenges of bureaucratic autonomy and democratic accountability
Role of civil services in policy formulation and implementation
Representation and diversity in civil services

topics.primary_source: 2nd ARC Report 2: Refurbishing Personnel Administration - Analysis of generalist tradition vs specialized expertise requirement
key_concepts: Generalist tradition - Indian civil service heritage, broader perspective
Generalist advantages - flexibility, wider understanding, adaptability
Generalist limitations - insufficient technical depth, complex policy errors
Specialist requirements - technical domains need domain expertise
Specialist advantages - deeper knowledge, better implementation, quality
Specialist risks - narrow perspective, lack of policy breadth
Functional specialization - creating specialized cadres within IAS
Technical bureaucracy - IIFC, forest service, engineering services
Inter-cadre coordination - specialists working under generalist administrators
International models - US civil service, UK system comparisons
Hybrid approaches - specialist tracks within general cadre
Training for generalists - how to develop technical understanding
Lateral entry - specialized professionals from private sector
Policy formulation - generalist role in translating specialist input

scheme_1.name: Indian Agricultural Service, Indian Forest Service (Specialized cadres)
scheme_1.objective: Specialized administration in agriculture and forestry sectors
scheme_1.status: Ongoing - parallel to generalist IAS cadre

scheme_2.name: Lateral Entry for IAS
scheme_2.objective: Bring specialized expertise into generalist cadre
scheme_2.status: Implemented - appointments made annually in select ministries

issues_challenges: Career progression barriers for specialists within generalist system
Coordination issues between generalists and specialists
Dual hierarchies creating confusion and conflicts
reforms_needed: Create clearer functional specialization track within civil service
Ensure specialist participation in policy formulation
Value technical expertise in performance appraisal
mains_answer_angles: Historical context of generalist tradition in Indian civil service
Adequacy of generalist approach for modern governance challenges
Case for specialized expertise in technical domains
Integrating generalists and specialists - best practices
Impact on policy quality and implementation effectiveness

topics.topic_name: APAR System - Performance Appraisal of Civil Servants
topics.primary_source: 2nd ARC Report 2: Refurbishing Personnel Administration - Need for objective, transparent performance assessment
key_concepts: APAR (Annual Performance Appraisal Report) - annual evaluation of officers
Confidentiality issue - appraisals kept secret from appraisees
Reporting officers - immediate superior provides assessment
Countersigning authority - review and verification of reports
Performance criteria - competence, ability, character, conduct, health
Rating system - numerical scale, comparative ranking
Self-assessment - officer's own evaluation (limited use)
Visibility period - year-long observation before assessment
Grievance redressal - limited appeal mechanisms against appraisals
Promotion implications - APAR scores influence career progression
Subjectivity concerns - potential for bias, favoritism
360-degree feedback - multi-rater approach (limited adoption)
Comparative ranking issues - zero-sum promotion competitions
Performance incentives - linking APAR to career advancement

scheme_1.name: APAR Reform Initiative
scheme_1.objective: Make APAR more transparent, objective, focused on competencies

scheme_2.name: Mission Karmayogi Competency Framework
scheme_2.objective: Develop competency-based appraisal linked to performance
scheme_2.status: Ongoing - integration with APAR under discussion

issues_challenges: Lack of transparency - officers not shown their appraisals
Compartmentalization - comparing officers across different roles
Morale impact - demotivation from subjective assessments
reforms_needed: Robust appeals - independent review of contested appraisals
Link to learning - development plans from performance gaps
mains_answer_angles: Current APAR system - strengths and limitations
Link between performance appraisal and career progression
Transparency vs confidentiality tension in appraisal systems
Role of appraisals in accountability and performance improvement

topics.topic_name: Right to Information Act 2005 - Provisions and Exemptions
topics.primary_source: 2nd ARC Report 11: Right to Information and Transparency in Governance - Framework for public access to government information
key_concepts: RTI Act 2005 - statutory right to information held by government agencies
Applicability - extends to central, state governments and public authorities
Application process - written request to Public Information Officer (PIO)
Timeline - 30 days for response, 48 hours for urgent matters
Fees - minimal charge, nominal rupee per page, appeals processing
Section 8 exemptions - information compromising sovereignty, national security
Section 8(1)(a) - national security and diplomatic relations
Section 8(1)(b) - strategic economic interests and trade secrets
Section 8(1)(d) - Cabinet papers and internal government deliberations
Section 8(1)(e) - information that violates privacy or causes bodily harm
Section 8(1)(j) - information in fiduciary relationship (banking, insurance)
Section 9 exemptions - information given in confidence by other entities
Privacy dimension - balancing transparency with personal data protection
Proactive disclosure - government's duty to proactively publish information
Third-party information - consent requirements before disclosure

scheme_1.name: Right to Information Act 2005
scheme_1.objective: Enable citizens to access government information, enhance transparency
scheme_1.status: Active - over 50 lakh applications annually across center and states

scheme_2.name: Open Government Data Portal
scheme_2.objective: Proactive disclosure of government datasets for public use

issues_challenges: Implementation gaps - slow responses, incomplete information provided
Exemption abuse - excessive use of Section 8 exemptions
Privacy conflicts - tension between transparency and data protection
Third-party issues - difficulty in handling third-party information
Capacity constraints - insufficient PIO training and resources
reforms_needed: Strict timelines enforcement - penalties for delays
Narrow exemptions - interpretation against public interest
Proactive disclosure - more government information published upfront
Appeal mechanisms - streamlined, faster appellate process
Capacity building - training for PIOs and appeals officers
mains_answer_angles: RTI as transparency mechanism and its role in accountability
Balance between public interest and legitimate exemptions
Impact of RTI on government functioning and citizen participation
Integration with data protection frameworks - DPDP Act 2023

topics.topic_name: RTI Amendment 2019 and Information Commissioner Tenure Controversy
topics.primary_source: 2nd ARC Report 11: Right to Information - Strength of Information Commission as independent institution
key_concepts: RTI Amendment 2019 - controversial changes to Information Commission functioning
CIC tenure change - fixed 5-year term instead of until age 65
Removal provisions - amendments to dismissal procedures
Independence implications - reduced security of tenure affects decisions
Finance Commission impact - government control over salary structure
Debate on amendments - criticism from transparency advocates
Counter-arguments - need for reviewing commission's decisions
Comparative analysis - tenure provisions in other countries
Impact on RTI culture - chilling effect on information disclosure
Subsequent developments - continuation of controversy
State Information Commissions - less affected, more variation
Judicial intervention - challenges in higher courts
International criticism - pressure from transparency organizations
Reversibility - calls for amendments to restore independence

scheme_1.name: RTI Amendment 2019
scheme_1.objective: Regulatory changes to Information Commission structure
scheme_1.status: Implemented - remains controversial, challenged in courts

issues_challenges: Uncertainty about career security affects decision-making
Increased political influence over transparency decisions
reforms_needed: Restore enhanced tenure security for Information Commissioners
Transparent appointment process with multi-stakeholder involvement
mains_answer_angles: Independence of Information Commissions - importance and threats
Impact on RTI effectiveness and transparency culture
Tension between government oversight and institutional autonomy
Comparative position with other constitutional institutions

topics.primary_source: 2nd ARC Report 1: Good Governance and Probity in Public Life - Institutional mechanisms for public probity
key_concepts: Lokpal concept - parliamentary ombudsman for government corruption
Lokayukta - state-level anti-corruption authority
Lokpal Act 2013 - statutory framework for central Lokpal
Applicability - Lokpal investigates Prime Minister, ministers, MPs, civil servants
Structure - Lokpal Chairperson plus members (judges, civil servants, eminent persons)
Jurisdiction conflicts - coordination with CBI, state authorities
Prosecution procedures - cases referred to CBI for investigation
Powers - investigation authority, complaint receiving, report submission
Complaints process - public complaints about corruption
Immunity provisions - protection from baseless complaints
Bench strength - inadequate number of benches affecting case disposal
Lokayukta establishment - state version with varying frameworks
Delay issues - long investigation and prosecution timelines
Comparison with Ombudsman - different models in different countries

scheme_1.name: Lokpal Institution
scheme_1.objective: Independent mechanism to investigate corruption by government officials
scheme_1.status: Operational - yet to achieve full bench strength, pending criminal prosecutions

scheme_2.name: Lokayukta Institutions
scheme_2.objective: State-level parallel to Lokpal for investigating state government corruption
scheme_2.status: Operational in most states - varying efficiency and resources

issues_challenges: Bench strength shortage - delays in case processing
CBI referral system - creates gaps, prosecution delays
Immunity implications - officers may take actions with impunity
reforms_needed: Enhance Lokpal and Lokayukta bench strength
Direct prosecution authority - reduce CBI reliance
mains_answer_angles: Lokpal as institutional mechanism for fighting corruption
Comparison of Lokpal and Lokayukta - effectiveness differences
Limitations of current framework - bench strength, prosecution delays
Role in ensuring accountability of government officials

topics.topic_name: CAG - Audit, Accountability and Parliamentary Role
topics.primary_source: 2nd ARC Report 1: Good Governance - CAG as institutional pillar of accountability
key_concepts: CAG (Comptroller and Auditor General) - constitutional authority (Article 148)
Appointment - by President on recommendation of Prime Minister
Tenure - 6 years or until age 65, whichever earlier
Independence - protected through constitutional provisions
Functions - audit of accounts, legality check, propriety audit
Financial audit - verification of government expenditure legitimacy
Compliance audit - checking adherence to rules and procedures
Performance audit - efficiency and effectiveness of programs
Standards - follows INTOSAI (International Organization of Supreme Audit Institutions)
Reporting - submits reports to Parliament, published as public documents
Parliamentary scrutiny - reports examined by PAC (Public Accounts Committee)
Enforcement - recommendations but not direct enforcement authority
Limitations - forensic investigation beyond scope, depends on agencies
Evolution - expanded scope from financial to performance auditing

scheme_1.name: CAG Institution
scheme_1.objective: Ensure public accountability through independent audit of government accounts
scheme_1.status: Operational - constitutional authority, reports tabled in Parliament

issues_challenges: Delayed reporting - time lag between audit and parliamentary discussion
Non-implementation - recommendations often not acted upon
Limited follow-up - no mechanism to ensure corrective action
Resource constraints - insufficient audit staff for vast government
Scope limitations - cannot investigate criminal aspects
reforms_needed: Faster reporting cycles - reduce audit-to-report timelines
Mandatory action plans - government must respond to recommendations
Follow-up mechanism - track implementation of CAG recommendations
Capacity enhancement - audit staff and technical resources
Coordination with anti-corruption agencies - CAG-CBI-CID linkage
mains_answer_angles: CAG as pillar of parliamentary accountability
Evolution from financial to performance audit
Effectiveness of audit system in ensuring government accountability
Challenges in CAG implementation and remedies

topics.topic_name: Digital India Programme and e-Governance Models
topics.primary_source: 2nd ARC Report 15: e-Governance for Improved Service Delivery - G2C, G2B, G2G, G2E service delivery transformation
key_concepts: G2C (Government-to-Citizen) services - bill payment, licenses, certificates
G2B (Government-to-Business) - registration, permits, compliance
G2G (Government-to-Government) - inter-ministry data sharing
G2E (Government-to-Employee) - payroll, benefits, leave management
Digital India 9 pillars - broadband, literacy, services, e-governance
UMANG platform - 1400+ services, single portal access
Aadhaar integration - unique ID enabling seamless service access
Mobile governance - m-governance expanding through smartphones
AI and automation - predictive analytics, process automation
Blockchain applications - transparency and immutability
Cloud infrastructure - scalable, cost-effective government IT
Cybersecurity hardening - data protection, vulnerability management
Digital literacy - capacity building for citizens and officials
Service quality benchmarking - citizen satisfaction metrics

scheme_1.name: Digital India Programme
scheme_1.objective: Digitally empowered society through broadband, literacy, e-services

scheme_2.name: UMANG Portal
scheme_2.objective: Single access point for 1400+ government services

topics.primary_source: 2nd ARC Report 15: e-Governance - Unique identification as service delivery enabler with privacy safeguards
key_concepts: Aadhaar - 12-digit unique identity for 130+ crore people
UIDAI authority - issuance, security, grievance redressal
Biometric authentication - iris, fingerprint collection
JAM Trinity - Jan Dhan, Aadhaar, Mobile financial inclusion
DBT system - Direct Benefit Transfer using Aadhaar
SC 2018 judgment - balance privacy with necessity
Section 57 controversy - private sector access restrictions
Linkage mandates - bank, PAN, mobile, ration card integration
Authentication failures - exclusion of poor in service delivery
Data security - CIDR encryption, breach incidents
DPDP Act 2023 - data protection provisions and exemptions
Consent mechanisms - explicit opt-in requirements
International models - comparison with other ID systems
Surveillance risks - potential for state tracking

scheme_1.name: Aadhaar Identification
scheme_1.objective: Unique ID for service delivery and financial inclusion

scheme_2.name: JAM Trinity
scheme_2.objective: Financial inclusion through account-identity-mobile nexus

topics.topic_name: Welfare Design - Universalism vs Targeting and Leakages
topics.primary_source: 2nd ARC Report 4: Social Capital - A Shared Destiny - Framework for inclusive welfare delivery with minimal wastage
key_concepts: Universal approach - covering all, ensuring certainty, reducing stigma
Targeting approach - concentrating on poorest, fiscal efficiency
Design trade-offs - cost vs coverage comprehensiveness
Inclusion-exclusion errors - false positives and negatives
Leakage sources - corruption, misadministration, design gaps
MGNREGA - universal employment guarantee model
PDS - mixed universal-targeted food security
Technology reduction - Aadhaar, DBT limiting corruption
Last-mile delivery - beneficiary awareness and access barriers
Intermediary discretion - local officials' gatekeeping role
Fiscal sustainability - balancing welfare and budget
Hybrid models - combining universal and targeted elements
Monitoring mechanisms - tracking benefit reach
Social audit - beneficiary participation and accountability

scheme_1.name: MGNREGA
scheme_1.objective: Guarantee rural employment - universal right-based approach

scheme_2.name: Public Distribution System
scheme_2.objective: Subsidized food security - mixed approach

topics.primary_source: 2nd ARC Report 14: Protecting Children and Public Health - Comprehensive access to essential services
key_concepts: RTE Act 2009 - constitutional right to free, compulsory education 6-14
Samagra Shiksha - integrated school education scheme
NEP 2020 - 5+3+3+4 structural reform
PM POSHAN - nutrition scheme with mid-day meals
Teacher quality - DIET training, NCTE standards
Digital learning - AI, MOOCs, online education
Assessment reform - continuous evaluation over board exams
Ayushman Bharat PMJAY - 5 lakh health coverage
HWCs - Health and Wellness Centers primary care
CDSCO - drug regulation and quality assurance
NHM - National Health Mission coordination
Mental Health Act 2017 - mental healthcare as right
NMC 2019 - replaced MCI with autonomous regulation
Telemedicine - remote service delivery expansion

scheme_1.name: Samagra Shiksha
scheme_1.objective: Integrated school education from pre-primary to secondary

scheme_2.name: Ayushman Bharat
scheme_2.objective: Health coverage and wellness centers

topics.primary_source: 2nd ARC Report 13: Separated and Marginalised Groups - Constitutional protections for historical remediation
key_concepts: Article 15 - non-discrimination on caste grounds
Article 15(4) - special provisions for SC/ST advancement
Article 16(4) - public employment reservation
Article 17 - untouchability abolition
Article 46 - state duty for SC/ST welfare
Article 338 - National Commission for SCs
Article 338A - National Commission for STs
Article 338B - National Commission for OBCs
Reservation scope - services, education, promotions
50% ceiling - total reservation cap (with exceptions)
Mandal Commission - 27% OBC reservation
Indra Sawhney judgment - constitutional validation
103rd Amendment - 10% EWS reservation
Creamy layer - income exclusion criteria

scheme_1.name: SC/ST Reservation
scheme_1.objective: Constitutional reservation for SC/ST advancement

scheme_2.name: National Commission for SCs/STs
scheme_2.objective: Investigate grievances, review programs

topics.primary_source: 2nd ARC Report 13: Separated Groups - Backward classes identification and socioeconomic advancement
key_concepts: Mandal Commission 1980 - backward classes survey
27% OBC reservation - public services and education
Backward class definition - educational, economic, social criteria
2600+ castes - OBC list notification
Creamy layer concept - exclusion of advanced members
Mandal implementation 1990 - social upheaval, protests
Indra Sawhney judgment - upheld quota with creamy layer
Income thresholds - periodic updates needed
State variations - TN 69%, some states lower
Private sector - excluded from OBC benefits
Inter-OBC conflicts - status competition within castes
Promotion quota - limited SC/ST/OBC advancement
103rd Amendment - EWS 10% for economically weaker
Grievance mechanisms - NCOB investigation powers

scheme_1.name: OBC Reservation System
scheme_1.objective: 27% reservation for socioeconomically backward classes

scheme_2.name: National Commission for OBCs
scheme_2.objective: Statutory commission for OBC grievances and list management

topics.primary_source: 2nd ARC Report 14: Protecting Children - Comprehensive framework for gender justice and women's safety
key_concepts: Domestic Violence Act 2005 - civil and criminal remedies
POSH Act 2013 - workplace sexual harassment prevention
Dowry Prohibition Act 1961 - regulates dowry practice
Pre-conception Pre-natal Diagnostic Act 1994 - gender-selective abortion prevention
Women's Reservation Act 2023 - 106th Amendment for 33% parliamentary seats
National Commission for Women - grievance investigation, advocacy
One-stop centers - integrated support for violence survivors
Beti Bachao Beti Padhao - girl child protection and education
Mahila Shakti Kendra - women's empowerment at grassroots
Sexual harassment definition - expanding scope of workplace protections
Victim support services - counseling, legal aid, rehabilitation
Intersectionality - addressing discrimination by caste, religion, sexuality
Implementation gaps - low conviction rates in gender violence cases
Awareness campaigns - behavior change communication

scheme_1.name: Women's Reservation Act 2023
scheme_1.objective: 33% reservation in Lok Sabha and state assemblies

scheme_2.name: Beti Bachao Beti Padhao
scheme_2.objective: Girl child protection, education, economic independence

topics.primary_source: 2nd ARC Report 14: Protecting Children - Comprehensive Perspective - Rights-based approach to child welfare
key_concepts: POCSO Act 2012 - sexual offenses against children

Health and nutrition - ICDS, nutrition programs
PENCIL portal - child labor monitoring system
Rehabilitation services - trauma counseling, vocational training
Prevention strategies - awareness, community mobilization
Data collection - improving information on child abuse
